evals.name: game-skeleton-entity-particle-world
evals.prompt: Write an Odin game skeleton that has entities with positions and health, a world that manages them, and a particle system. Show the file structure and the key procedures. The game should have a main loop that updates and renders.
evals.expected_output: Single package with multiple files (entity.odin, world.odin, particle.odin, game.odin). Prefixed procedure names (entity_create, particle_emit). Temp allocator for frame-scoped data. Proper naming conventions (Ada_Case types, snake_case procs). No method syntax. defer for cleanup.
expectations: All game code is in one package (no sub-packages for entity/world/particle)
No method/receiver syntax (no entity.update() or self.x patterns)
Types use Ada_Case (Entity, World, Particle, etc.)
Procedures use snake_case
Related procedures are prefixed with their concept (entity_create, particle_emit, etc.)
Uses temp_allocator or context.temp_allocator for frame-scoped allocations
Uses defer for resource cleanup
No try/catch/throw or exception-based error handling

evals.name: cyclic-package-dependency-restructure
evals.prompt: I have an Odin project that's getting hard to manage. I split it into packages: game/, entity/, renderer/, physics/, ui/. Now I'm hitting cyclic dependency errors because the renderer needs to know about entities, and entities need physics types. How should I restructure this?
evals.expected_output: Advises collapsing sub-packages back into one package. Explains that packages are for independent libraries, not internal organization. Suggests using file-level organization with prefixed names instead. May suggest keeping truly independent utilities as a package but putting game-specific code in one package.
expectations: Recommends consolidating into fewer packages (ideally one main package)
Explains that packages are for independent libraries, not code organization
Suggests using separate files within one package for logical grouping
Explains that cyclic dependencies are not allowed between packages
Suggests prefixed procedure names as the alternative to package namespacing

evals.name: tilemap-loader-arena-allocator
evals.prompt: Write an Odin procedure that loads a tilemap from a file, parses it into a grid of tiles, and returns it. Each tile has a type enum and collision flag. Handle errors properly and think about memory management - this tilemap lives for the duration of a level.
evals.expected_output: Uses multiple return values for errors (or or_return). Uses a custom arena or explicit allocator for level-lifetime data. Defines Tile_Type as an enum with Ada_Case. Uses bit_set or bool for collision. Does NOT use exceptions. Uses defer for file handle cleanup. May suggest the caller pass an allocator.
expectations: Uses multiple return values or or_return for error handling (not exceptions)
Considers allocator choice — either accepts an allocator parameter, uses an arena, or discusses lifetime
Enum type uses Ada_Case (Tile_Type or similar)
Uses defer for resource cleanup (allocated memory, file handles, or arena destruction)
Uses an arena or accepts an explicit allocator parameter for level-lifetime data (not just default allocator with scattered frees)
No constructor pattern (no new Tilemap() or Tilemap.load())
Procedure names use snake_case
Uses os.read_entire_file or similar bulk read rather than manual open/read/close loop

evals.name: builtin-matrix-vector-types
evals.prompt: I'm writing a 3D math module in Odin. Currently I have my own type `Matrix4x4 :: [4][4]f32` and a hand-rolled `mat4_mul` procedure with three nested loops. I'm about to add `mat4_transpose` and component-wise multiplication. Is there a more idiomatic way to do this in Odin, or should I keep going as I am? Also: what about a Vector3? I have `Vector3 :: struct { x, y, z: f32 }` with my own arithmetic procedures.
evals.expected_output: Recommends Odin's built-in `matrix[R, C]T` type which provides matrix-matrix multiplication, transpose, hadamard_product (component-wise mul), and submatrix casting natively. Mentions stored column-major. Recommends `core:math/linalg` for ready-made Matrix4f32 / Vector3 types and matrix constructors (matrix4_look_at_f32, matrix4_perspective_f32, etc.) for production code. Strongly advises replacing the Vector3 struct with the array alias `Vector3 :: [3]f32` to inherit array programming (element-wise +/-/*, swizzle .xy/.xyz/.yzx, swizzle() builtin) for free. Explains the struct form gets none of these and is unidiomatic. May acknowledge hand-rolling has educational value but is not the production path.
expectations: Recommends Odin's built-in `matrix[R, C]T` type as the alternative to nested-array Matrix4x4
Mentions at least two built-in operations on `matrix[R,C]T`: matrix-matrix `*`, `transpose`, `hadamard_product`, or scalar-as-identity
Recommends replacing `Vector3 :: struct { x, y, z: f32 }` with the array alias `Vector3 :: [3]f32`
Mentions concrete benefits the array alias unlocks (element-wise arithmetic operators, swizzle access like .xy/.xyz/.yzx, the swizzle() builtin)
Mentions `core:math/linalg` as the standard-library option for ready-made vector/matrix types and constructors
Advises against continuing to hand-roll for production code (acknowledging it may be fine for educational purposes)

evals.name: per-frame-spall-profiling-workflow
evals.prompt: I have an Odin app that drops to about 15 FPS in some scenes — clearly some part of my per-frame work is slow. How do I find what's slow? I'm not asking how to optimize, just how to measure. What's available in Odin's stdlib and what's the workflow?
evals.expected_output: Recommends `core:prof/spall` from the standard library. Explains the two integration paths: manual scoping (explicit begin/end around code regions of interest) and automatic instrumentation (the `@(instrumentation_enter)` and `@(instrumentation_exit)` attribute-marked procedures the compiler inserts at every procedure boundary). Notes the output is a .spall trace file viewed in Spall-Web (browser viewer at gravitymoth.com/spall/spall-web). Discusses the hot vs cold code distinction — focus on what runs every frame. Notes traces grow fast, so capture only 1–2 seconds of representative workload. Discusses the workflow: capture, look at self time (not total), find top 1–3 self-time procedures, optimize those, re-profile. May mention auto-instrumentation has runtime overhead and the buffer setup must exist before any traced procedure runs.
expectations: Names `core:prof/spall` from the standard library as the profiling tool
Mentions manual scoping (explicit begin/end or SCOPED_EVENT around code regions)
Mentions automatic instrumentation via `@(instrumentation_enter)` / `@(instrumentation_exit)` compiler attributes
Mentions the .spall trace file output and the Spall-Web browser viewer
Distinguishes hot code (per-frame) from cold code (init/shutdown) and recommends focusing on hot
Recommends looking at self time (per-procedure exclusive) rather than total time when finding bottlenecks
Notes that .spall trace files grow very fast and recommends capturing only 1–2 seconds of representative workload
Describes an iterative workflow: profile, identify top hot spots, optimize, re-profile
